meta.description: Phase 1 hand-built Kant cluster + Phase 3 backbone (bb-v1) over the full Ethics registry

nodes.label: Categorical Imperative
nodes.definition: Kant's supreme principle of morality: act only according to that maxim whereby you can at the same time will that it should become a universal law. Unconditional, applying to all rational agents regardless of their desires.

nodes.label: Hypothetical Imperative
nodes.definition: A command of reason that applies only conditionally, given some desired end: 'if you want X, do Y'. Contrasted by Kant with the unconditional categorical imperative.

nodes.label: Autonomy
nodes.definition: Self-legislation of the rational will: the capacity to act according to laws one gives oneself, rather than from external causes or inclination. For Kant, the ground of human dignity.

nodes.label: Deontology
nodes.definition: A normative ethical theory that judges the morality of actions by their conformity to duties or rules, rather than by their consequences.

nodes.label: Consequentialism
nodes.definition: The family of normative ethical theories holding that the moral rightness of an act depends only on the value of its consequences.

nodes.label: Utilitarianism
nodes.definition: The consequentialist theory that the right action is the one that maximises overall well-being or utility, classically 'the greatest happiness of the greatest number'.

nodes.label: Virtue Ethics
nodes.definition: A normative approach centring on character and the virtues — stable excellences of the person — rather than on rules or consequences. Rooted in Aristotle's eudaimonism.

nodes.label: Normative Ethics
nodes.definition: The branch of moral philosophy that studies what makes actions right or wrong and what we ought to do, comprising principally deontology, consequentialism, and virtue ethics.

nodes.label: Immanuel Kant
nodes.definition: German philosopher (1724–1804), central figure of modern philosophy. Author of the three Critiques; founder of deontological ethics grounded in the categorical imperative and autonomy of the will.

nodes.label: David Hume
nodes.definition: Scottish empiricist philosopher (1711–1776). His scepticism about causation and reason famously woke Kant from his 'dogmatic slumber'.

nodes.label: Jeremy Bentham
nodes.definition: English philosopher and reformer (1748–1832), founder of classical utilitarianism and the principle of utility.

nodes.label: John Stuart Mill
nodes.definition: English philosopher (1806–1873) who refined utilitarianism with qualitative distinctions among pleasures, and defended liberty and representative government.

nodes.label: Aristotle
nodes.definition: Greek philosopher (384–322 BC). His Nicomachean Ethics grounds virtue ethics in eudaimonia — flourishing achieved through excellent activity of the soul.

nodes.label: German Idealism
nodes.definition: The philosophical movement (c. 1781–1840) emerging from Kant's critical philosophy, developed by Fichte, Schelling, and Hegel.

nodes.label: Free Will
nodes.definition: ability of agents to be the ultimate source or originator of their choices, free from external determination

nodes.label: Determinism
nodes.definition: philosophical belief that all events are determined completely by previously existing causes

nodes.label: Compatibilism
nodes.definition: belief that free will and determinism are mutually compatible to one another

nodes.label: Thomas Hobbes
nodes.definition: Thomas Hobbes was an English philosopher (1588-1679) best known for his political philosophy and theories about human nature and the social contract. His ideas profoundly influenced subsequent philosophical thought across multiple disciplines.

nodes.label: Moral Responsibility
nodes.definition: status of morally deserving praise, blame, reward, or punishment for an act or omission, in accordance with one's moral obligations

nodes.label: Moral Luck
nodes.definition: A situation where a person can be held morally responsible or judged for things that are partly influenced by circumstances beyond their control, creating tension with the principle that morality should only assess what we can control.

nodes.label: Bernard Williams
nodes.definition: A British philosopher who explored the tension between moral responsibility and factors beyond individual control, demonstrating through his work that people are often held morally accountable for outcomes influenced by chance or circumstances rather than purely by their choices and intentions.

nodes.label: Moral Status
nodes.definition: description of intrinsic self-worth and dignity

nodes.label: Moral Agency
nodes.definition: ability to make ethical judgements

nodes.label: Applied Ethics
nodes.definition: branch of philosophy

nodes.label: Trolley Problem
nodes.definition: thought experiment in ethics

nodes.label: Moral Dilemma
nodes.definition: decision-making problem between two conflicting moral imperatives, neither of which is unambiguously acceptable or preferable

nodes.label: Philippa Foot
nodes.definition: English philosopher (1920–2010)

nodes.label: Doctrine of Double Effect
nodes.definition: set of ethical criteria permitting certain actions when one's otherwise legitimate act may also cause harm

nodes.label: Thomas Aquinas
nodes.definition: Italian Dominican friar, philosopher, Catholic priest, and Doctor of the Church (1225–1274)

nodes.label: Natural Law
nodes.definition: system of law that is purportedly determined by nature, and is thus universal

nodes.label: Supererogation
nodes.definition: acts which are good but not morally required to be done

nodes.label: Duty
nodes.definition: legal or moral requirement to take a certain course of action

nodes.label: Practical Reason
nodes.definition: Practical reason is the mental capacity humans use to think through and decide what actions to perform by weighing reasons and considering different options.

nodes.label: Eudaimonia
nodes.definition: Eudaimonia is an ancient Greek concept referring to the highest human good, often understood as happiness, flourishing, or living well through the development and exercise of virtue and excellence of character.

nodes.label: Well-Being
nodes.definition: measure of how well life is to someone or a group with factors such as health, happiness and satisfaction

nodes.label: Justice
nodes.definition: broad idea of a situation where people receive that which they deserve

nodes.label: Virtue
nodes.definition: morally positive trait or quality deemed to be morally good

nodes.label: Rights
nodes.definition: fundamental legal, social, or ethical principles of freedom or entitlement according to some legal system, social convention, or ethical theory

nodes.label: Peter Singer
nodes.definition: Peter Singer is a contemporary Australian-American philosopher and ethicist known for applying consequentialist reasoning to practical moral issues, particularly regarding animal welfare, global poverty, and bioethics.

nodes.label: Psychological Egoism
nodes.definition: The view that true altruism in humans is impossible

nodes.label: Altruism
nodes.definition: principle or practice of concern for the welfare of others

nodes.label: Socrates
nodes.definition: 5th-century BCE Greek philosopher

nodes.label: Akrasia
nodes.definition: lack of self-control or state of acting against one's better judgment

nodes.label: Metaethics
nodes.definition: branch of ethics seeking to understand ethical properties

nodes.label: Contractualism
nodes.definition: A moral theory holding that an action is wrong if it violates principles that rational people could not reasonably reject as a fair basis for governing everyone's behavior.

nodes.label: Scholasticism
nodes.definition: method of critical thought which dominated teaching by the academics ("scholastics", or "schoolmen") of medieval universities in Europe from about 1100 to 1700

nodes.label: Hedonism
nodes.definition: A philosophical view holding that pleasure and pain are the fundamental forces governing human behavior and determining what has value.

nodes.label: Universalizability
nodes.definition: concept in Kantian ethics

nodes.label: Prescriptivism
nodes.definition: metaethical view that ethical sentences function similarly to imperatives which are universalizable—whoever makes a moral judgment is committed to the same judgment in any situation where the same relevant facts obtain

nodes.label: Moral Realism
nodes.definition: Moral realism is the philosophical view that moral claims, such as statements about right and wrong, describe actual facts about the world in the same way that factual claims describe other aspects of reality, and that at least some of these moral claims are genuinely true.

nodes.label: Moral Anti-Realism
nodes.definition: Moral anti-realism is the philosophical view that moral properties, facts, or values do not exist independently of human minds and judgments.

nodes.label: Moral Cognitivism
nodes.definition: the meta-ethical view that ethical sentences express propositions and can therefore be true or false

nodes.label: Non-Cognitivism
nodes.definition: the meta-ethical view that ethical sentences do not express propositions and thus cannot be true or false

nodes.label: Emotivism
nodes.definition: metaethical view that moral statements are expressions of emotional attitudes rather than objective truths

nodes.label: Expressivism
nodes.definition: metaethical theory that moral sentences are not descriptive, but express an evaluative attitude toward an object

nodes.label: A. J. Ayer
nodes.definition: A. J. Ayer was a British philosopher known for developing and defending emotivism, a non-cognitivist theory of ethics that interprets moral statements as expressions of emotional attitudes rather than factual claims about the world.

nodes.label: Charles Stevenson
nodes.definition: Charles Stevenson was a philosopher known for developing emotivism, a non-cognitivist theory of ethics that argues moral statements express emotional attitudes rather than factual claims about moral properties.

nodes.label: R. M. Hare
nodes.definition: R. M. Hare was a British moral philosopher known for developing prescriptivism, a theory that moral judgments function as universal prescriptions or commands rather than factual statements about objective properties.

nodes.label: Simon Blackburn
nodes.definition: A British philosopher known for developing quasi-realism, an approach to moral philosophy that explains how moral language can function meaningfully and appear truth-based even though moral statements do not express straightforward factual claims about objective moral properties.

nodes.label: Allan Gibbard
nodes.definition: American philosopher and social choice theorist

nodes.label: Quasi-Realism
nodes.definition: A philosophical approach that attempts to preserve the language and structure of moral realism while rejecting the claim that moral properties exist independently of human attitudes, seeking to show how moral statements can be meaningful and truth-apt without requiring objective moral facts.

nodes.label: Error Theory
nodes.definition: Error theory is the position that moral judgments make claims about objective facts in the world, but these claims are systematically false because no objective moral properties or facts actually exist.

nodes.label: J. L. Mackie
nodes.definition: J. L. Mackie was a philosopher known for developing the error theory of morality, which argues that our moral judgments attempt to describe objective moral facts, but such facts do not actually exist in the world.

nodes.label: Ethics: Inventing Right and Wrong
nodes.definition: A 1977 philosophical work by J.L. Mackie arguing that moral values and duties do not exist as objective features of reality, but are human inventions or projections without independent factual basis.

nodes.label: Moral Nihilism
nodes.definition: meta-ethical view that nothing is intrinsically moral or immoral

nodes.label: Moral Subjectivism
nodes.definition: meta-ethical view

nodes.label: Moral Relativism
nodes.definition: philosophical positions about the differences in moral judgments across peoples and cultures

nodes.label: Moral Naturalism
nodes.definition: metaethical position

nodes.label: Moral Non-Naturalism
nodes.definition: Moral non-naturalism is the philosophical view that moral properties—such as goodness or rightness—exist as real features of the world but are fundamentally different from and cannot be reduced to natural properties studied by science.

nodes.label: G. E. Moore
nodes.definition: G.E. Moore was an influential British philosopher whose ethical theory in his work Principia Ethica became the foundational model for understanding moral non-naturalism, the view that moral properties exist independently of physical or natural properties.

nodes.label: Moral Intuitionism
nodes.definition: Moral intuitionism is a philosophical view that holds we can directly grasp certain basic moral truths without reasoning or argument, and that moral properties belong to a non-natural realm distinct from physical facts about the world.

nodes.label: H. A. Prichard
nodes.definition: British philosopher (1871–1947)

nodes.label: W. D. Ross
nodes.definition: Scottish philosopher and translator (1877–1971)

nodes.label: Naturalistic Fallacy
nodes.definition: ethical argument asserting that it is fallacious to explain something good reductively

nodes.label: Open Question Argument
nodes.definition: philosophical argument put forward by British philosopher G. E. Moore, to refute the equating of the property of goodness with some non-moral property, X, whether naturalistic (e.g. pleasure) or supernatural

nodes.label: Prima Facie Duty
nodes.definition: A principle that identifies morally relevant considerations favoring or opposing a particular action, without determining what one ought to do overall.

nodes.label: Moral Sentimentalism
nodes.definition: A philosophical view holding that emotions, feelings, and desires are fundamental to how we understand and judge morality, rather than reason alone.

nodes.label: Francis Hutcheson
nodes.definition: An 18th-century Irish-Scottish philosopher who developed early theories of moral sentimentalism, arguing that human emotions and feelings are fundamental to understanding morality rather than reason alone.

nodes.label: Rationalism
nodes.definition: philosophical view that reason should be the chief source of knowledge

nodes.label: Is-Ought Problem
nodes.definition: philosophical problem articulated by David Hume in 1739 about how one can deduce prescriptive statements (what ought to be) from descriptive statements (what is)

nodes.label: Moral Particularism
nodes.definition: theory in meta-ethics

nodes.label: Act Utilitarianism
nodes.definition: A moral theory holding that an action is right when it produces consequences at least as beneficial for overall human well-being as any other action available to the agent, making it the most direct approach to evaluating individual acts through their utility-maximizing outcomes.

nodes.label: Rule Utilitarianism
nodes.definition: form of utilitarianism that says an action is right as it conforms to a rule that leads to the greatest good

nodes.label: Henry Sidgwick
nodes.definition: A 19th-century British philosopher known for his significant contributions to utilitarian ethical theory, which evaluates actions based on their consequences and the overall happiness they produce.

nodes.label: Greatest Happiness Principle
nodes.definition: A principle stating that moral and legal decisions should aim to maximize the total happiness of all members of a community rather than merely the greatest number, ensuring that minority interests are not sacrificed for majority benefit.

nodes.label: Heteronomy
nodes.definition: philosophical concept

nodes.label: Good Will
nodes.definition: A disposition or intention to act in accordance with moral duties and principles rather than consequences; the quality of being motivated by what one believes to be ethically right regardless of outcomes.

nodes.label: Kingdom of Ends
nodes.definition: Kant’s thought experiment about a world in which all humans are treated as ends, not as means

nodes.label: Rosalind Hursthouse
nodes.definition: A contemporary philosopher specializing in virtue ethics, the approach to morality that centers on character and virtues rather than duties or consequences. Hursthouse has made significant contributions to developing and defending virtue ethical theory as a major framework in normative ethics.

nodes.label: Alasdair MacIntyre
nodes.definition: Alasdair MacIntyre is a philosopher who appears in discussions of virtue ethics, particularly regarding how moral character and virtues function as foundational concepts in ethical theory rather than being defined through other moral frameworks.

nodes.label: Elizabeth Anscombe
nodes.definition: Elizabeth Anscombe was a philosopher who revitalized virtue ethics in Western philosophy through her influential 1958 article "Modern Moral Philosophy," which challenged prevailing ethical theories and restored focus to character, virtues, and how people should live.

nodes.label: Golden Mean
nodes.definition: The golden mean is the ethical principle that virtue lies between two extremes—one of excess and one of deficiency.

nodes.label: Practical Wisdom
nodes.definition: ancient Greek word for a type of wisdom or intelligence

nodes.label: Contractarianism
nodes.definition: Contractarianism is a political and moral theory asserting that governmental authority and ethical norms derive their legitimacy from the agreement or mutual consent of the people involved, rather than from divine authority or human nature.

nodes.label: Social Contract
nodes.definition: concept in political philosophy

nodes.label: T. M. Scanlon
nodes.definition: T. M. Scanlon is a contemporary philosopher who represents the Kantian strain of social contract thought, known as contractualism, which emphasizes that moral and political principles must be justifiable to all individuals based on respect for persons rather than self-interest.

nodes.label: John Rawls
nodes.definition: A nineteenth-century British philosopher who made major contributions to utilitarian and liberal philosophy, particularly through his works on ethics and political theory that emphasized human happiness and individual freedom as foundational principles for evaluating actions and institutions.

nodes.label: Ethical Egoism
nodes.definition: ethical position that moral agents should act in their own self-interest

nodes.label: Epicurus
nodes.definition: An ancient Greek philosopher who advocated that pleasure and the absence of pain constitute the highest good, and whose ideas significantly influenced philosophical debates about the nature of human motivation and what makes life worth living.

nodes.label: Divine Command Theory
nodes.definition: theory that morality is commanded by, and originates only from, the divine

nodes.label: Ethics of Care
nodes.definition: An ethical approach emphasizing relationships, interdependence, and attentiveness to the concrete needs of individuals within their specific contexts, rather than abstract universal rules or principles.

nodes.label: Carol Gilligan
nodes.definition: American feminist, ethicist, and psychologist

nodes.label: Nel Noddings
nodes.definition: Nel Noddings is a philosopher who developed an influential ethical framework centered on caring relationships and interpersonal responsibility, arguing that morality should prioritize responding to the needs of those we are connected to rather than following abstract universal rules.

nodes.label: Feminist Ethics
nodes.definition: Feminist ethics is a philosophical approach that examines how gender shapes moral beliefs, practices, and ethical theory, while working to identify and correct gendered oppression and the ways gender hierarchies harm people, particularly women and marginalized groups.

nodes.label: Arthur Schopenhauer
nodes.definition: A 19th-century German philosopher and cultural critic known for his radical attacks on traditional morality, religion, and conventional thinking, who developed psychological theories to expose hidden assumptions in established values and proposed alternative frameworks for human flourishing and…

nodes.label: Friedrich Nietzsche
nodes.definition: Friedrich Nietzsche was a 19th-century German philosopher and cultural critic who fundamentally challenged traditional European morality, religion, and conventional thought through psychological analysis, while proposing alternative values aimed at cultural transformation and human flourishing.

nodes.label: Derek Parfit
nodes.definition: Derek Parfit was a British philosopher known for his contributions to ethics, metaphysics, and practical reason, particularly his work on personal identity, rationality, and what makes actions worth performing.

nodes.label: Plato
nodes.definition: Plato was an ancient Greek philosopher and one of the founding figures of virtue ethics in Western philosophy, who emphasized moral character and virtuous excellence as central to ethical life.

nodes.label: Christine Korsgaard
nodes.definition: American philosopher

nodes.label: Stoicism
nodes.definition: school of Hellenistic philosophy who held that the practice of virtue suffices to attain eudaimonia

nodes.label: Zeno of Citium
nodes.definition: Greek philosopher, founder of Stoicism

nodes.label: Epicureanism
nodes.definition: philosophical movement developed by Epicurus

nodes.label: Empiricism
nodes.definition: theory that states that knowledge comes only or primarily from sensory experience

nodes.label: Joseph Butler
nodes.definition: Joseph Butler was an 18th-century English philosopher known for his influential critique of psychological egoism—the theory that people are motivated solely by self-interest.

nodes.label: Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals
nodes.definition: Translation of Kant's Groundwork published in 1997

nodes.label: Critique of Practical Reason
nodes.definition: 1788 work by Immanuel Kant

nodes.label: Critique of Pure Reason
nodes.definition: A foundational work of modern philosophy by Immanuel Kant that examines how human knowledge is constructed and what we can actually know about the world.

nodes.label: The Metaphysics of Morals
nodes.definition: work of political and moral philosophy by Immanuel Kant

nodes.label: Nicomachean Ethics
nodes.definition: A philosophical treatise by Aristotle examining human flourishing, virtue, and the qualities needed for living well, which treats ethics as a practical discipline focused on developing character through habit and deliberation rather than abstract theoretical knowledge.

nodes.label: Republic
nodes.definition: philosophical work written by Plato

nodes.label: Leviathan
nodes.definition: A philosophical work by Thomas Hobbes presenting a theory of ethics and politics grounded in natural law, which argues that self-preservation is the fundamental human good and that divine laws of nature guide human behavior toward this end.
aliases: Leviathan or The Matter, Forme and Power of a Commonwealth Ecclesiasticall and Civil

nodes.label: A Treatise of Human Nature
nodes.definition: A philosophical work by David Hume examining the foundations of human nature, particularly focusing on how moral judgments arise from sentiments and emotions rather than reason alone, and exploring the distinction between natural and artificial virtues.

nodes.label: An Enquiry Concerning the Principles of Morals
nodes.definition: An 18th-century philosophical work by David Hume that presents his theory of morality, arguing that moral judgments arise from human sentiment and feeling rather than reason alone, and examining how virtues and vices develop in society through sympathy and social convention.

nodes.label: An Introduction to the Principles of Morals and Legislation
nodes.definition: intro to the Principles of Morals and Legislation

nodes.label: Utilitarianism (Mill, 1861)
nodes.definition: A philosophical work by John Stuart Mill published in 1861 that examines the ethical theory that actions and policies should be judged by whether they maximize overall happiness or well-being, arguing that pleasure and the absence of pain are the primary measures of what is good.

nodes.label: On Liberty
nodes.definition: A philosophical work by John Stuart Mill that examines the principles governing individual liberty and the limits of social authority, arguing that personal freedom should be maximized except when one's actions directly harm others.

nodes.label: The Methods of Ethics
nodes.definition: A philosophical work by Henry Sidgwick published in 1874 that develops an intuitional utilitarian ethical theory, examining how moral principles and concepts of utility can guide human conduct and decision-making.

nodes.label: Principia Ethica
nodes.definition: A foundational 1903 philosophical work by G.E. Moore that argues moral properties like goodness are non-natural and self-evident, knowable through intuition rather than empirical observation or logical proof.

nodes.label: Language, Truth and Logic
nodes.definition: philosophical book by A. J. Ayer

nodes.label: The Right and the Good
nodes.definition: 1930 book by the Scottish philosopher David Ross

nodes.label: A Theory of Justice
nodes.definition: work of political philosophy and ethics by John Rawls

nodes.label: After Virtue
nodes.definition: A influential 1981 philosophical work by Alasdair MacIntyre that critiques modern ethics and argues that moral reasoning lacks objective foundations because it has become disconnected from its historical contexts and the virtues that once grounded it.

nodes.label: Reasons and Persons
nodes.definition: A major philosophical work by Derek Parfit examining how reasons, moral facts, and personal identity shape our obligations to ourselves and others, particularly regarding population ethics and future generations.

nodes.label: What We Owe to Each Other
nodes.definition: A philosophical work by Harvard philosopher T. M. Scanlon that develops a theory of morality based on principles that no one could reasonably reject as a basis for mutual agreement.

nodes.label: On the Genealogy of Morality
nodes.definition: essay by Friedrich Nietzsche

nodes.label: Modern Moral Philosophy
nodes.definition: A philosophical work by Elizabeth Anscombe published in 1958 that challenged prevailing approaches to ethics by arguing for renewed attention to virtue and moral character, catalyzing a major revival of virtue ethics as a central framework in Western moral philosophy.

nodes.label: slave_revolt_in_morality
nodes.definition: Nietzsche's historical thesis that modern morality originated when the powerless oppressed developed a resentful hatred of their masters and inverted values, labeling the masters' traits (strength, domination) as evil an

nodes.label: master_morality
nodes.definition: The original form of moral evaluation based on the good/bad distinction, arising from the self-affirmation and sense of superiority of the strong and noble. Values strength, courage, excellence, and integrity over univer

evidence.quote: Immanuel Kant (1724–1804) argued that the supreme principle of morality is a principle of rationality that he dubbed the “Categorical Imperative” (CI).
Kant believes that our moral concerns are dominated by the question of what duties are imposed on us by a law that commands with a uniquely moral necessity.
At the heart of Kant’s moral theory is the idea of autonomy.

A hypothetical imperative is a command that also applies to us in virtue of our having a rational will, but not simply in virtue of this. It requires us to exercise our wills in a certain way given we have antecedently willed an end. A hypothetical imperative is thus a command in a conditional form.
deontology is one of those kinds of normative theories regarding which choices are morally required, forbidden, or permitted
Virtue ethics is currently one of three major approaches in normative ethics.

Although Kant gives several examples in the Groundwork that illustrate the CI, he goes on to describe in later writings, especially in The Metaphysics of Morals, a complicated normative ethical theory for interpreting and applying the CI to human persons in ordinary contexts.
Consequentialism, as its name suggests, is simply the view that normative properties depend only on consequences.
within the domain of moral theories that assess our choices, deontologists—those who subscribe to deontological theories of morality—stand in opposition to consequentialists
Virtue ethics is currently one of three major approaches in normative ethics.
The approach is a species of consequentialism, which holds that the moral quality of an action or policy is entirely a function of its consequences, or the value produced by the action or policy.
Virtue ethics is currently one of three major approaches in normative ethics.
within the domain of moral theories that assess our choices, deontologists—those who subscribe to deontological theories of morality—stand in opposition to consequentialists
the one that emphasizes the virtues, or moral character, in contrast to the approach that emphasizes duties or rules (deontology) or that emphasizes the consequences of actions (consequentialism).
The paradigm case of consequentialism is utilitarianism
Utilitarians, for example, identify the Good with pleasure, happiness, desire satisfaction, or “welfare” in some other sense
A utilitarian will point to the fact that the consequences of doing so will maximize well-being
What distinguishes utilitarianism from other forms of consequentialism are specific commitments regarding the nature of the value to be produced as well as the approach to that value.
it was Bentham who rendered the theory in its recognisably secular and systematic form
The paradigm case of consequentialism is utilitarianism, whose classic proponents were Jeremy Bentham (1789), John Stuart Mill (1861), and Henry Sidgwick (1907).
Jeremy Bentham asserted both psychological and ethical hedonism with the first two sentences of his book An Introduction to the Principles of Morals and Legislation
Mill was raised in the tradition of Philosophical Radicalism, made famous by Jeremy Bentham (1748–1832)
Though the first systematic account of utilitarianism was developed by Jeremy Bentham (1748–1832), the core insight motivating the theory occurred much earlier.
As is well known, while adhering to the basic Benthamic analysis of motives, in Utilitarianism (1861) J. S. Mill introduced the concept of“higher pleasures”
The paradigm case of consequentialism is utilitarianism, whose classic proponents were Jeremy Bentham (1789), John Stuart Mill (1861), and Henry Sidgwick (1907).
J.S. Mill (ch. 2) developed an alternative approach according to which there is ‘higher’ and ‘lower’ pleasure, and its value is irreducibly a matter of its quality as well as its quantity.
Though Mill accepts the utilitarian legacy of the Radicals, he transforms that legacy in important ways.

Because Bentham, quite famously, had an enormous impact on the development of John Stuart Mill’s thought, it seemed natural to view the development of Classical Utilitarianism as proceeding from Bentham through Mill.
Aristotle follows Socrates and Plato in taking the virtues to be central to a well-lived life.
In the West, virtue ethics’ founding fathers are Plato and Aristotle

Kant credited Hume with waking him from his “dogmatic slumber”, and he describes the Critique of Pure Reason, arguably the most important work of modern philosophy, as the solution to the “Humean problem in its greatest possible amplification”
Thus, at the heart of Kant’s moral philosophy is a conception of reason whose reach in practical affairs goes well beyond that of a Humean ‘slave’ to the passions.
It was in the flush of his early commitment to utilitarianism that Mill edited the five volumes of Bentham’s writings on evidence
The paradigm case of consequentialism is utilitarianism, whose classic proponents were Jeremy Bentham (1789), John Stuart Mill (1861), and Henry Sidgwick (1907).
John Stuart Mill (1806–1873) was a follower of Bentham, and, through most of his life, greatly admired Bentham’s work even though he disagreed with some of Bentham’s claims
Compatibilists maintain that free will and moral responsibility are compatible with determinism.
Kant sides with Hume and Hutcheson against the psychological egoism associated with Pufendorf, Hobbes, Locke, and Mandeville

philosophers in the Modern period (such as Hobbes and Hume) distinguished the general way in which our actions are necessitated if determinism is true from the specific instances of necessity sometimes imposed on us by everyday constraints on behavior
Kant was directly influenced by the sentimentalist tradition to which Hume belongs (especially Francis Hutcheson’s version).

This compatibilist tradition was carried into the 20th century by logical positivists such as Ayer (1954) and Schlick ([1930]1966). Here is how Schlick expressed a central compatibilist insight in 1930 (drawing, in particular, on Hume)
Bernard Williams writes, “when I first introduced the expression moral luck, I expected to suggest an oxymoron”
Thomas Aquinas is credited with introducing the principle of double effect in his discussion of the permissibility of self-defense in the Summa Theologica (II-II, Qu. 64, Art.7).
Deontologists of this stripe are committed to something like the doctrine of double effect, a long-established doctrine of Catholic theology (Woodward 2001)

supererogation, the category of actions that are praiseworthy (either in creating good states of affairs or in reflecting a particularly virtuous trait of character) yet at the same time not obligatory
justice, charity, courage, and generosity are virtues

Such norms are to be simply obeyed by each moral agent; such norm-keepings are not to be maximized by each agent. In this sense, for such deontologists, the Right is said to have priority over the Good
Metaethical moral relativist positions are typically contrasted with moral objectivism.
non-naturalism is a form of moral realism

Moral realism is not a particular substantive moral view nor does it carry a distinctive metaphysical commitment over and above the commitment that comes with thinking moral claims can be true or false and some are true.
So understood, moral anti-realism is the disjunction of three theses: moral noncognitivism moral error theory moral non-objectivism
moral anti-realism is the denial of the thesis that moral properties—or facts, objects, relations, events, etc. (whatever categories one is willing to countenance)—exist objectively.

non-cognitivists argue that Moore’s mistake was in thinking that moral claims attribute any sort of property to things, and so he was also wrong in thinking that moral claims have propositional content and express genuine beliefs
Moral noncognitivism holds that our moral judgments are not in the business of aiming at truth.
Other views—variously called moral non-cognitivism, expressivism, anti-realism, nihilism, etc.—contend that moral judgments lack truth-value
There are broadly two ways of endorsing (1): moral noncognitivism and moral error theory.
those who reject moral realism are usefully divided into (i) those who think moral claims do not purport to report facts in light of which they are true or false (noncognitivists)
Emotivists think moral terms in grammatically assertive utterances function primarily to express emotion and perhaps also to elicit similar emotions in others

recent versions of expressivism, flying the banner of “quasi-realism”, have taken as a central project explaining, on non-cognitivist foundations, how and why moral language has all the trappings of being cognitivist and realist
Another influential kind of noncognitivism called “prescriptivism” claims that moral judgments are really veiled commands

Noncognitivists often appeal to this apparent contrast to argue that moral claims have this necessary connection to motivation precisely because they do not express beliefs (that might be true or false) but instead express motivational states of desire, approval, or commitment

Emotivists think moral terms in grammatically assertive utterances function primarily to express emotion and perhaps also to elicit similar emotions in others (Barnes 1933; Stevenson 1946; Ayer 1952, Chapter 6).

Some theorists who view themselves as emotivists suggest that even the most general terms of moral evaluation have a descriptive meaning rather than just an emotive or non-cognitive meaning (Stevenson 1944, 22; Hare 1952, suggests the same sort of idea within a prescriptivist theory at 118).
R.M. Hare (1952, 1963) restricted this to commands that one is willing to universalize.
By contrast current versions of prescriptivism, most developed in the works of R. M. Hare, have attempted to vindicate moral thinking as a rational enterprise.

Simon Blackburn, whose quasi-realist project was briefly described above, has contributed various ideas not only for the states expressed by indicative sentences but also for complex embeddings of moral claims.
But the proponent who has developed the program in the most systematic way is Allan Gibbard.
Simon Blackburn, for example, has pursued what he calls a “quasi-realist” program (Blackburn 1984, 1993, 1998).
‘Quasi-Realism’ is Simon Blackburn’s name for this sort of non-cognitivism, and especially his own version of expressivism.

recent versions of expressivism, flying the banner of “quasi-realism”, have taken as a central project explaining, on non-cognitivist foundations, how and why moral language has all the trappings of being cognitivist and realist

The quasi-realist is someone who endorses an anti-realist metaphysical stance but who seeks, through philosophical maneuvering, to earn the right for moral discourse to enjoy all the trappings of realist talk.

What especially distinguishes the quasi-realist project is an emphasis on explaining why we are entitled to act as if moral judgments are genuinely truth-apt even while strictly speaking they are neither true nor false in any robust sense.
Defenders of moral error theory include Mackie 1977, Hinckfuss 1987, Joyce 2001, and Olson 2014.
John Mackie (see Mackie 1977 and Joyce 2001)
Some error theorists do argue that combining cognitivism with motivational internalism results in an untenable position (Mackie 1977).
John Mackie (who coined the term “error theory” in 1977) argues that when we participate in moral discourse we commit ourselves to the existence of objective values and objective prescriptions
non-naturalism is a form of moral realism and is opposed to non-cognitivist positions
there is widespread agreement that G.E. Moore’s account of goodness in Principia Ethica is a paradigmatically non-naturalist account
This standard view can be traced to a powerful and influential argument offered by G.E. Moore (1903).

Moore offered a simple test. Take whichever account you will—say, one according to which to be good is to be pleasant—and then consider whether a person who understands the terms involved might nonetheless intelligibly ask whether something she acknowledges to be pleasant is good.
Moore famously claimed that naturalists were guilty of what he called the “naturalistic fallacy.”
This standard view can be traced to a powerful and influential argument offered by G.E. Moore (1903).
Moore’s open question argument can be regarded as giving form to this intuition.

Moore offered a simple test. Take whichever account you will—say, one according to which to be good is to be pleasant—and then consider whether a person who understands the terms involved might nonetheless intelligibly ask whether something she acknowledges to be pleasant is good.
Moore’s “Open Question Argument” for the conclusion that goodness is a non-natural property
Ross writes, a self-evident proposition is “evident without any need of proof, or of evidence beyond itself” (1930/2002, 29)

the roots of the modern sentimentalist tradition in ethics go back to early 18th century debates in Britain. Anthony Ashley Cooper, better known by his title as the Earl of Shaftesbury, introduced the notion of a moral sense

Hume sides with the moral sense theorists: we gain awareness of moral good and evil by experiencing the pleasure of approval and the uneasiness of disapproval when we contemplate a character trait or action from an imaginatively sensitive and unbiased point of view.
Kant was directly influenced by the sentimentalist tradition to which Hume belongs (especially Francis Hutcheson’s version).
Following Hutcheson, Hume rejects reason or reasoning as the source of moral distinctions
in contrast to the approach that emphasizes duties or rules (deontology) or that emphasizes the consequences of actions (consequentialism).
in contrast to the approach that emphasizes duties or rules (deontology) or that emphasizes the consequences of actions (consequentialism).
Act Utilitarianism: An act is right insofar as its consequences for the general happiness are at least as good as any alternative available to the agent.
Rule Utilitarianism: An act is right insofar as it conforms to a rule whose acceptance value for the general happiness is at least as great as any alternative rule available to the agent.
The paradigm case of consequentialism is utilitarianism, whose classic proponents were Jeremy Bentham (1789), John Stuart Mill (1861), and Henry Sidgwick (1907).
Other key contributors to debate over hedonism include Plato, Aristotle, Epicurus, Aquinas, Butler, Hume, Mill, Nietzsche, Brentano, Sidgwick, Moore, Ross, Broad, Ryle and Chisholm.
Sidgwick was also a British philosopher, and his views developed out of and in response to those of Bentham and Mill.
Advocates of these theories often call them consequentialism rather than utilitarianism so that their theories will not be subject to refutation by association with the classic utilitarian theory.
In the Fragment Bentham stated the “fundamental axiom” that “it is the greatest happiness of the greatest number that is the measure of right and wrong”
Classical Utilitarianism emerged in the nineteenth-century and is distinguished from earlier forms of utilitarianism by it’s clear structure and committment to hedonism as a theory of value.
If any philosopher is regarded as central to deontological moral theories, it is surely Immanuel Kant

At the heart of Kant’s moral theory is the idea of autonomy. Understanding the idea of autonomy was, in Kant’s view, key to understanding and justifying the authority that moral requirements have over us.
Kant holds that the fundamental principle of our moral duties is a categorical imperative.
For Kant, the only thing unqualifiedly good is a good will (Kant 1785)
Kant’s analysis of commonsense ideas begins with the thought that the only thing good without qualification is a “good will”.
Kant’s notion of autonomy is one of the more central, distinctive, and influential aspects of his ethics.

Kant states that the above concept of every rational will as a will that must regard itself as enacting laws binding all rational wills is closely connected to another concept, that of a “systematic union of different rational beings under common laws”, or a “Kingdom of Ends” (G 4:433).
It was heralded by Anscombe’s famous article “Modern Moral Philosophy” (Anscombe 1958)
We begin by discussing two concepts that are central to all forms of virtue ethics, namely, virtue and practical wisdom.
These are arête (excellence or virtue), phronesis (practical or moral wisdom) and eudaimonia
These are arête (excellence or virtue), phronesis (practical or moral wisdom) and eudaimonia (usually translated as happiness or flourishing).
every ethical virtue is a condition intermediate (a “golden mean” as it is popularly known) between two other states, one involving excess, and the other deficiency
Following (and adapting) Aristotle, virtue ethicists draw a distinction between full or perfect virtue and “continence”
Another way in which one can easily fall short of full virtue is through lacking phronesis—moral or practical wisdom.
In VII.1–10 Aristotle investigates character traits—continence and incontinence—that are not as blameworthy as the vices but not as praiseworthy as the virtues.
Contractarianism, which stems from the Hobbesian line of social contract thought, holds that persons are primarily self-interested
Contractualism, which stems from the Kantian line of social contract thought, holds that rationality requires that we respect persons
It has been more recently recognized that there are two distinct strains of social contract thought, which now typically go by the names contractarianism and contractualism.
Other key contributors to debate over hedonism include Plato, Aristotle, Epicurus, Aquinas, Butler, Hume, Mill, Nietzsche, Brentano, Sidgwick, Moore, Ross, Broad, Ryle and Chisholm.
Aquinas says that the fundamental principle of the natural law is that good is to be done and evil avoided (ST IaIIae 94, 2).

Psychologist Carol Gilligan’s landmark work, In a Different Voice: Psychological Theory and Women’s Development (1982), disputes accounts of moral development that do not take into account girls’ moral experiences
Nel Noddings’s influential work, Caring: A Feminine Approach to Ethics and Moral Education (1984), argues for the moral preferability of a care perspective
The ethic of care has its roots in projects that aim to correct for the exclusion of women from traditional theorizations of moral reasoning.
he was already interested in philosophy, particularly the work of Arthur Schopenhauer and Friedrich Albert Lange
Sidgwick was also a British philosopher, and his views developed out of and in response to those of Bentham and Mill.
In the West, virtue ethics’ founding fathers are Plato and Aristotle

These writers, not surprisingly, trace their views to Aquinas as the major influence, though they do not claim to reproduce his views in detail. (See, for example, Grisez 1983, Finnis 1980, MacIntyre 1999, and Murphy 2001.)
It has also generated virtue ethical readings of philosophers other than Plato and Aristotle, such as Martineau, Hume and Nietzsche
Hume roundly criticizes Hobbes for his insistence on psychological egoism or something close to it
Kant was directly influenced by the sentimentalist tradition to which Hume belongs (especially Francis Hutcheson’s version).
Hume was heavily influenced by Hutcheson, who was one of his teachers.
modern virtue ethics does not have to take a “neo-Aristotelian” or eudaimonist form
It has also generated virtue ethical readings of philosophers other than Plato and Aristotle, such as Martineau, Hume and Nietzsche, and thereby different forms of virtue ethics have developed
Such views ascribe to us very demanding requirements of impartial benevolence, which leave little scope for the independent significance of the agent’s own projects and interests (Williams 1973).

In The Methods Sidgwick is concerned with developing an account of “…the different methods of Ethics that I find implicit in our common moral reasoning…” These methods are egoism, intuition based morality, and utilitarianism.
A common objection to psychological egoism, made famously by Joseph Butler, is that I must desire things other than my own welfare in order to get welfare.
For Kant, the only thing unqualifiedly good is a good will (Kant 1785)
Kant: A good will is not good because of what it effects or accomplishes, because of its fitness to attain some proposed end, but only because of its volition, that is, it is good in itself
Kant reports that his “labor” in the Critique of Pure Reason was fundamentally a response to “that Humean skeptical teaching” (CPrR 5:32).
In the Metaphysics of Morals, Kant argues that additional feelings also play a role in motivating virtuous conduct.
Aristotle wrote two ethical treatises: the Nicomachean Ethics and the Eudemian Ethics.
A picture along these lines can be found in Aristotle’s suggestion (in Book III of the Nicomachean Ethics) that one can be responsible for being a careless person

He held that the laws of nature are divine law (Leviathan, xv, ¶41), that all humans are bound by them (Leviathan, xv, ¶¶36), and that it is easy to know at least the basics of the natural law (Leviathan, xv, ¶35).
Hume’s main ethical writings are Book 3 of his Treatise of Human Nature, “Of Morals” (which builds on Book 2, “Of the Passions”)

There were certainly occasional discussions of moral disagreement—for example in Michel de Montaigne’s Essays or in the dialogue David Hume attached to An Enquiry Concerning the Principles of Morals.

In An Introduction to the Principles of Morals and Legislation (printed 1780, published with additions 1789), as a preliminary to developing a theory of penal law he detailed the basic elements of classical utilitarian theory.
Bentham wrote, “It is not to be expected that this process [his hedonic calculus] should be strictly pursued previously to every moral judgment.” (1789, Chap. IV, Sec. VI)

Jeremy Bentham asserted both psychological and ethical hedonism with the first two sentences of his book An Introduction to the Principles of Morals and Legislation: “Nature has placed mankind under the governance of two sovereign masters, pain, and pleasure.
Bentham, Jeremy, 1789 [PML]. An Introduction to the Principles of Morals and Legislation, Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1907.
J.S. Mill (ch. 2) developed an alternative approach according to which there is ‘higher’ and ‘lower’ pleasure
–––, 1859. On Liberty, London: Longman, Roberts & Green.

James Fitzjames Stephen, Henry Sidgwick, and A.V. Dicey all advocated versions of utilitarian individualism, although Sidgwick occasionally gave voice to “socialist” sentiments in developing his intuitional utilitarian theory in The Methods of Ethics (1874).
Sidgwick added, “It is not necessary that the end which gives the criterion of rightness should always be the end at which we consciously aim.” (1907, 413)
Sidgwick thought that good could be analysed as what ought to be desired
Henry Sidgwick (1838–1900), for one, read Mill as a psychological egoist (The Methods of Ethics 42–44).
A very similar argument was used by Sidgwick to establish that certain moral notions are irreducible (see Sidgwick 1907: Book I, Chapter 3)

Henry Sidgwick’s (1838–1900) The Methods of Ethics (1874) is one of the most well known works in utilitarian moral philosophy, and deservedly so.

At the beginning of the 20th Century, G. E. Moore’s open question argument convinced many philosophers that moral statements were not equivalent to statements made using non-moral or descriptive terms.

Moore is the intuitionist who laid most stress on the non-natural nature of moral properties, though his focus was on goodness rather than rightness. In Principia Ethica Moore defines a natural property
Moore famously claimed that naturalists were guilty of what he called the “naturalistic fallacy.”
G. E. Moore (1873–1958) criticized this as fallacious. He argued that it rested on an obvious ambiguity: Mill has made as naïve and artless a use of the naturalistic fallacy as anybody could desire.

It was heralded by Anscombe’s famous article “Modern Moral Philosophy” (Anscombe 1958) which crystallized an increasing dissatisfaction with the forms of deontology and utilitarianism then prevailing.
